parsed_target.game_explanation: This game likely centers on red-for-blue bargaining, because red is privately most valuable to everyone at $1.00 while blue differs meaningfully across players: otter values BLUE at $0.40, peacock at $0.50, and cat at $0.70. Green is a constant $0.50 for all, so it is less useful for creating private gains except as a neutral balancing chip. From an analyst view, mutually beneficial trades exist when lower-blue-value players give up BLUE to receive RED, while higher-blue-value players may be more willing to surrender RED only if they can get enough BLUE in return. However, during play each person knows only her own values, so they must infer others' willingness from observed acceptances and holdings. Because everyone values RED highly, proposals demanding RED in exchange for too little will usually fail. Otter should be the most eager buyer of RED using BLUE, since BLUE is cheapest for her. Cat should be the least eager to give away BLUE cheaply because BLUE is relatively valuable to her, but she may still accept trades that improve her own calculator. Peacock sits in the middle and, given her lower norm-enforcement and inequality tolerance, may accept modest positive-surplus trades rather than hold out. Personas also suggest relatively structured, cautious bargaining rather than wild experimentation: early proposals may test rates, then later turns adapt toward simple positive-surplus exchanges. Since multiple acceptors are resolved randomly, proposers have some incentive to post offers attractive to either counterpart, but because values are fairly aligned on RED and GREEN, many successful deals should be straightforward bilateral-looking blue-for-red trades. A plausible full path is that early round-0 proposals reveal resistance to weak asks for RED, after which peacock and otter shift toward clearer positive-value offers, with cat selectively accepting when the terms are good enough.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no revealed bargaining history, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative. Because each player only knows her own calculator, early offers may test whether others seem willing to part with RED or BLUE at favorable ratios. Weak attempts to obtain RED cheaply are especially likely to be declined because all players value RED highly.

By round 1, one accepted green-for-blue trade has shown that otter is willing to move BLUE when profitable and that peacock will make simple positive-calculator offers. Players should now have slightly better beliefs about who may be flexible. Otter is the natural candidate to propose a clearer blue-for-red trade, while cat remains likely to reject giving up RED unless compensated strongly.

In the final round, there is no future strategic value to signaling, so proposals should focus on immediate calculator gains. Peacock moves first and may improve on her earlier failed red request by offering a bit more. Otter, having already succeeded once in converting BLUE into RED, is likely to try another direct version. Cat, proposing last, may make a mild, relationship-preserving offer, but with no further rounds and limited private-gain opportunities, a final declined proposal is plausible.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should be driven by sharp bilateral gains from moving RED toward badger-red-5032, moving BLUE toward badger-red-5032, and moving GREEN away from everyone only when it helps finance those trades. Publicly, everyone knows only that RED and BLUE could each be worth anywhere from $0.10 to $1.00 to others, while GREEN is fixed at $0.50. Privately, badger values RED and BLUE equally highly at $0.80, camel values RED at $0.60 and BLUE at $0.40, and squid values BLUE at $0.60 and RED very poorly at $0.20. That means the strongest true gains from trade are: squid should be willing to give up RED for BLUE or GREEN; camel should be somewhat willing to give up BLUE for RED or GREEN; and badger should want both RED and BLUE, especially by paying with GREEN if possible. Because acceptance is simultaneous and multiple acceptors create random matching, proposers have an incentive to post offers that are clearly attractive to at least one likely partner rather than highly selective offers. In the first game of the session, players are likely to be somewhat cautious and use simple, easy-to-evaluate ratios. The main uncertainty is that each player must infer others' values from responses and realized trades, so early proposals may mix exploration with surplus seeking. Given the personas, all three are reasonably cooperative and cognitively strong, so the forecast is for mostly individually profitable, not spiteful, bargaining: early probing offers, then better targeted exchanges once trade patterns reveal who likely values which color.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is likely to be exploratory. With no history yet, players do not know which colors others truly value, so proposals should use intuitive terms that are favorable for the proposer and at least plausibly acceptable to one responder. Badger, who values both RED and BLUE highly and has a stable, reasoned style, is likely to test whether anyone will give up a valuable color for GREEN. Camel and squid then get to react both to the offer itself and to whether either responder accepts, which begins revealing private valuations.

After round 0, the revealed pattern is informative. Squid accepted GREEN for RED, suggesting low RED value, and badger accepted RED-for-BLUE, suggesting high RED value relative to BLUE. Camel's failed attempt to buy BLUE with GREEN indicates others are not eager to part with BLUE at a one-for-one green-equivalent rate. So round 1 likely becomes more targeted: badger presses further for RED from squid; camel tries to convert low-value BLUE into RED from badger if possible; squid may continue unloading RED for something he values more.

By the final round, trade patterns strongly suggest that squid is the cheap source of RED, badger is the eager buyer of RED and also willing to move BLUE at balanced terms, and camel values RED more than BLUE but not enough to overpay. Final-round behavior should therefore focus on simple surplus extraction rather than long-run signaling. Players are also constrained by updated holdings, so offers likely use colors they still hold in sufficient quantity and avoid ambitious proposals that would just be rejected.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game is likely driven by a clear but only partially inferable comparative-advantage structure. Dog values RED most ($1.00) and BLUE least ($0.20), butterfly values BLUE most ($0.90) and RED moderately ($0.60), and ram values GREEN at the common $0.50 but has especially low values for BLUE ($0.10) and only modest value for RED ($0.40). Since GREEN is worth the same to everyone, it often serves as a relatively transparent exchange medium, while RED and BLUE create private gains from trade. From an analyst view, the strongest surplus opportunities are dog acquiring BLUE cheaply from others, butterfly acquiring RED from ram, and ram parting with BLUE for almost anything worthwhile. But during play each participant knows only their own values, so proposals must be based on inference from acceptance behavior and public holdings, not direct knowledge of others' payoffs. The personas suggest all three are fairly tolerant of unequal deals and not especially punitive, so exploitative-but-still-acceptable offers are plausible. Dog's structure-seeking and social adaptiveness make her likely to open with a simple, conservative trade that is clearly profitable to herself and plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart. Butterfly's strong reasoning and stability make him likely to search for efficient terms once he sees what gets accepted. Ram is comparatively resource-protective but not norm-enforcing, so he is likely to accept clean positive-surplus deals and propose straightforward exchanges out of low-value BLUE when possible. Overall, the likely path is that early offers test the market, then accepted trades reveal information and shift holdings, with the limited three-round horizon pushing players toward practical, individually profitable deals rather than elaborate signaling.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, uncertainty is highest because no one has yet observed others' willingness to trade. Proposers are likely to use simple, legible offers. Dog should want more BLUE and avoid giving up RED unless compensated very well; butterfly should try to gain RED or unload RED for BLUE only on favorable terms; ram should be most willing to give up BLUE and least willing to give up GREEN unless paid enough. Because responders see their own exact surplus, round-0 acceptance mostly depends on whether a posted trade is individually positive rather than on fairness concerns.

By round 1, the first two accepted exchanges would reveal that BLUE can move at terms favorable to the non-BLUE lovers, while butterfly's attempt to buy RED too cheaply would have failed. Dog now holds more BLUE and may continue converting low-value GREEN into BLUE if possible. Butterfly, having seen resistance on RED requests, is likely to make a more responder-friendly offer, especially toward ram, who values RED relatively little. Ram now has evidence that dog is a willing counterparty for BLUE-based swaps but may also recognize that butterfly could pay better for RED.

In the final round, strategic experimentation matters less because there is no future reputation to cultivate beyond the current game. Players should make direct surplus-seeking offers using the information embedded in prior acceptances. Dog is likely to continue trying to turn GREEN into BLUE or otherwise improve her portfolio without sacrificing much RED. Butterfly, now more informed, should target RED again if any low-value holder remains. Ram, after parting with some BLUE and RED, may either make one last simple positive trade or post a demanding offer that fails if his best bargaining opportunities are exhausted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear gains-from-trade structure driven by opposite private values for RED and BLUE, while GREEN is a neutral outside option worth $0.50 to everyone. Lion values BLUE most ($1.00) and RED only modestly ($0.40), so lion strongly wants to acquire BLUE and is relatively willing to part with RED or GREEN. Iguana values RED most ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.40), so iguana is the natural counterparty for RED-for-BLUE trades. Alligator values BLUE more than RED ($0.60 vs $0.30), but less extremely than lion, so alligator also prefers to obtain BLUE and shed RED. Because players do not know one another's exact values during play, proposals are likely to mix surplus-seeking with uncertainty management: offers need to look attractive enough that at least one recipient may accept, but not so generous that the proposer gives away too much. A key strategic friction is that a sender cannot target a specific recipient; if both other players accept, one is chosen at random. That makes broad offers risky when only one counterpart is likely to generate strong gains. In this lineup, the most likely successful bargains are offers of RED in exchange for BLUE that iguana accepts and the others reject. GREEN may be used for exploratory or conservative trades because it has known common value, but it creates weaker surplus than the RED-BLUE mismatch. Persona priors suggest alligator may begin cautiously and systematically, lion may be comparatively open to making efficient and generous-looking trades, and iguana may accept favorable unequal deals without strong fairness resistance. Overall, the likely path is early probing followed by one or more RED-for-BLUE exchanges, especially involving iguana on the receiving side of BLUE and giving RED, or vice versa depending on who proposes.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. With no revealed history yet, round 0 is the main discovery phase. Alligator is likely to open with a cautious, structured proposal that asks for BLUE and offers RED, since that is clearly good from her own calculator. Lion and iguana then update from observed willingness to trade those colors. Because both lion and alligator privately like BLUE better than RED, iguana is the only likely accepter of many RED-for-BLUE offers, although proposers do not know that for sure during play.

By round 1, public history suggests that offers requesting BLUE and paying RED can succeed, and specifically that iguana has already accepted such deals. Current holdings after round 0 are: alligator 7 RED, 12 BLUE, 10 GREEN; lion 6 RED, 7 BLUE, 10 GREEN; iguana 17 RED, 11 BLUE, 10 GREEN. Alligator now has even less RED and more BLUE, so she may slow down or test whether GREEN can substitute in trade. Lion still values BLUE enormously and has reason to continue offering RED for BLUE. Iguana now has a large RED inventory and may try to rebalance by selling RED for GREEN or BLUE, but counterparties will only accept if their own calculator shows gains.

Entering the final round, holdings are: alligator 7 RED, 12 BLUE, 10 GREEN; lion 3 RED, 9 BLUE, 10 GREEN; iguana 20 RED, 9 BLUE, 10 GREEN. The revealed pattern now strongly indicates that iguana is the willing BLUE seller in exchange for RED, while alligator is not. With only one round left, proposers have less reason to experiment and more reason to exploit the most plausible bilateral surplus. Alligator may make one more efficient RED-for-BLUE offer. Lion has very little RED left, so may either scale down the same trade or pause. Iguana, flush with RED and short on reasons to value BLUE, may try a modest RED-for-GREEN conversion to improve her own payoff, but others' calculators make acceptance unlikely unless the ratio is generous enough.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should center on red-blue exchange rather than green. Green is worth exactly $0.50 to everyone, so it serves mainly as a neutral filler and does not create much private-information advantage. The real gains from trade come from heterogeneous red and blue values: whale-red-86 values RED very highly ($1.00) and BLUE relatively low ($0.40); dolphin-orange-3122 values BLUE highly ($0.80) and RED only moderately ($0.60); duck-orange-4971 values RED highly ($1.00) and BLUE moderately ($0.60). From an analyst view, dolphin is the natural seller of RED and buyer of BLUE, while whale is the strongest seller of BLUE and buyer of RED. Duck also likes RED, so duck will usually resist giving up RED unless compensated generously, but duck may accept trades that convert some RED into enough BLUE or GREEN at a private gain. Because players only know their own values, they must infer willingness from observed accepts and declines. That creates uncertainty: whale may suspect others do not value RED much and try to buy it cheaply; dolphin may suspect others value BLUE enough to trade away RED; duck may make somewhat more accommodating or exploratory offers because of high cooperation cues and very low norm-enforcement. The acceptance rule also matters: if both non-senders would profit, either may accept and the recipient is random, so proposers have some incentive to post broadly acceptable offers. Across three rounds, likely behavior is a mix of exploratory first offers, then adjustment toward deals that are clearly profitable to at least one counterparty. A plausible full-game path is that whale first tries to acquire RED using BLUE, dolphin then successfully trades RED away for BLUE, duck tests whether whale will pay enough BLUE for RED, and later rounds continue with modest, individually profitable red-blue swaps. Overall, the likely pattern is several accepted trades between whale and dolphin, occasional duck participation, little use of green, and no strong fairness-based refusal of profitable but unequal-looking offers.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each color, but nobody has direct evidence yet about others' private values. Early proposals are likely to be somewhat diagnostic. Whale has the clearest incentive to ask for RED and offer BLUE, since that is strongly profitable for whale if priced favorably. Dolphin is the one player for whom giving up RED can be attractive, because RED is only worth $0.60 to dolphin while BLUE is worth $0.80. Duck also likes RED a lot, so duck should usually reject whale's attempts to buy RED unless the payment in BLUE is large. This round is therefore likely to reveal whether anyone is willing to move RED for BLUE and at what rough terms.

By round 1, public history has shown that dolphin is willing to trade for BLUE and that duck will not give away RED cheaply. Holdings have shifted: whale has less BLUE and more RED, dolphin has more RED than before and still likes BLUE most, and duck has increased BLUE at the cost of some RED. This makes another whale-to-dolphin red-for-blue trade especially plausible. Because accepted offers give information, later proposals are likely to be sharper and more personalized even though identities of acceptors are all that can be publicly inferred.

In the final round, there is no future strategic value to waiting, so proposals should focus on immediate private surplus. Whale still wants more RED if it can be obtained with BLUE at acceptable terms; dolphin still benefits from converting some RED into BLUE; duck remains less naturally matched with the others except when dolphin can be induced to swap. Final-round offers may therefore be a bit more generous or more direct, with less experimentation and more attempt to close any remaining profitable bilateral trade.

Because accept decisions are based on each responder's own visible surplus calculator, proposals that make at least one responder strictly better off are likely to be accepted, but proposers may initially test aggressive terms. The strongest hidden opportunity is that swan values RED much less than bear and peacock do, but no one knows that directly. Bear can update from that public history even without knowing exact values and may imitate that direction. By the final round, inventories have shifted enough to make some earlier trades less attractive. rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, nobody has revealed much, so proposals are likely to test the market while still guaranteeing the proposer a gain if accepted. Cat moves first and is the most flexible player, so an initial probing offer involving giving up RED or GREEN to request BLUE is plausible. Iguana and zebra then react using only their own values; because zebra highly values BLUE, zebra should reject giving BLUE cheaply, while iguana is more open to parting with BLUE. Iguana's own turn should then focus on acquiring RED, her most valuable chip, likely by offering BLUE, which she values least. In the final round, players no longer need to preserve future bargaining positions, so proposals can become more opportunistic and sharply tailored to immediate value. Final-round acceptance should still follow strict own-profit logic because there is no reason to accept a loss-making trade when no future reciprocity is available.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has symmetric endowments, so the main strategic task is price discovery. Bear is likely to open with a structured, conservative request for BLUE, since BLUE is bear's top-value chip. But bear may not want to part with much RED because RED is also valuable to bear. Peacock, who values BLUE least, is the most likely early seller of BLUE if offered RED or enough GREEN. Swan may also try to convert low-value RED into BLUE or GREEN, but swan's best private motive is to keep BLUE and shed RED.

After round 0, bear has already increased BLUE and reduced GREEN, peacock has increased GREEN and reduced BLUE, and swan has turned some RED into BLUE but then partly replenished through peacock's demand for RED. With revealed behavior suggesting that peacock will sell BLUE and accept RED-for-BLUE style trades, round 1 likely features more targeted proposals. Bear now has evidence that peacock can be a counterparty for BLUE acquisition, while swan has evidence that peacock values RED enough to trade for it.

By the final round, trade patterns are clearer. Peacock has repeatedly shown willingness to exchange away BLUE and to acquire RED when favorable. Bear has strong incentive to continue buying BLUE, while swan still benefits from converting RED into higher-value BLUE. But chip constraints now matter more, and players may become slightly more selective because there are only a few turns left to use acquired chips. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear efficiency pattern from the analyst's view, but the players face substantial private-information uncertainty. Gorilla values RED most highly ($1.00) and BLUE very little ($0.10), so she most wants to acquire RED and shed BLUE. Camel values BLUE relatively highly ($0.60), RED poorly ($0.30), and GREEN at the common $0.50, so he is the natural middle player who can benefit from giving up RED for BLUE but has weaker incentives to give away BLUE. Monkey values both RED ($1.00) and BLUE ($0.90) highly, so he dislikes parting with either and is the least naturally motivated to accept trades that require giving up those colors; GREEN is his only moderate-value asset. Publicly, everyone knows only that red and blue lie somewhere in the common range, so proposals must be based on guesses about others' values and willingness to accept. Because acceptances are individually value-transparent through the surplus calculator, players should almost never knowingly accept negative-surplus trades. That pushes proposers toward offers that are plausibly attractive to at least one opponent. A likely dynamic is that Gorilla opens with a conservative attempt to trade away BLUE for RED, since this is strongly beneficial for her and plausibly beneficial if someone else values BLUE enough. Camel, being analytical and not very generosity-oriented, is likely to propose relatively calculated trades that improve his own portfolio without giving away too much BLUE. Monkey is cooperative but also highly reflective; given his high values for both RED and BLUE, he is likely to use GREEN as the main thing he offers when he proposes. The main uncertainty is that players do not know who values which color most, so some early proposals may fail because the wrong counterparty would need to accept. Over three rounds, successful trades are most likely to involve Camel transferring RED for BLUE, and Monkey transferring GREEN in exchange for RED when that is favorable to Gorilla.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip. By the final round, players have observed repeated patterns and can better target feasible trades. The main uncertainty is adverse selection from simultaneous acceptance: if frog posts an offer attractive to both others, either could receive it at random, so proposers may target ratios that are acceptable mainly to one likely counterparty. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear value gradient that should shape bargaining. Given the personas, duck and lion are cautious, structured, and unlikely to make reckless self-sacrificing proposals; tiger is somewhat more flexible but still not likely to accept losses. parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear efficiency gradient in BLUE chips and a weaker one in RED chips. BLUE is worth $1.00 to ram-purple-2824, $0.60 to lion-blue-472, and only $0.10 to octopus-green-4361, so octopus is the natural seller of BLUE and ram is the natural buyer. RED is worth $0.50 to lion, $0.30 to ram, and $0.20 to octopus, so octopus is also the lowest-value holder of RED, while lion is the highest-value buyer. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it serves as a natural medium of exchange and a safe benchmark. Because each player only knows their own values during play, they cannot directly identify the efficient counterparties, but the on-screen surplus calculator lets each responder immediately see whether a posted trade helps them. That tends to eliminate loss-making acceptances. The main uncertainty is therefore on the proposal side: players may experiment with terms that are good for themselves but too demanding for others, especially early. Lion's cooperative and reflective profile makes moderately fair, executable offers more likely than extreme extraction. Octopus is also prosocial and inequality-tolerant, but highly reflective and structured, so he is likely to post simple trades that convert low-value BLUE or RED into GREEN or other chips. Ram is the most self-protective and least generous, so he is likely to make harder bargains, especially when trying to acquire BLUE-retaining or GREEN-gaining positions; however, since he values BLUE most, he is also selective about giving BLUE away. A likely full-game pattern is early discovery through accepted obviously profitable trades involving octopus as seller of BLUE, lion using GREEN or RED to improve her portfolio, and ram occasionally accepting when he can gain GREEN or shed lower-value RED. Multiple acceptance risk matters whenever octopus offers BLUE for GREEN, because both lion and ram benefit from receiving BLUE at a low enough price; when that happens, recipient selection is random among acceptors. Overall, the most likely trajectory is several accepted trades centered on octopus liquidating low-value BLUE, with fewer successful proposals from ram because his bargaining stance is tougher and his private incentives make him reluctant to part with highly valued BLUE.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip. No one has public information about others' values, so opening-round proposals are likely to test simple exchange rates against the constant-value GREEN benchmark. Since GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone, proposals involving GREEN are easiest to evaluate and most likely to be accepted if they clearly help the responder. Lion is likely to begin with a moderate trade that improves her holdings without seeming too aggressive; octopus has the strongest private incentive to unload BLUE because it is nearly worthless to him; ram is likely to be more demanding and less willing to sacrifice valuable BLUE.

After round 0, the public history reveals something about willingness to trade even though not about private values. Lion has successfully converted GREEN into BLUE once, suggesting openness to mutually beneficial exchange. Octopus has publicly offered a very generous BLUE-for-GREEN deal, which may indicate that he does not value BLUE highly, though others cannot be sure. Ram's declined offer suggests he may ask for steep terms. In this round, lion is likely to target RED or more BLUE if she can do so profitably, octopus may continue monetizing BLUE, and ram may adjust after seeing that aggressive terms fail.

By the final round, players have more evidence from behavior. Repeated acceptance of octopus's BLUE offers makes it increasingly plausible to others that BLUE is cheap for him, so final-round trades may continue to channel BLUE away from octopus. Lion's accepted trades suggest she is willing to make balanced proposals that create visible responder gains. Ram, after two failed attempts, may either soften terms or still overreach; given his resource-protective profile, a final demanding proposal remains plausible. Endgame incentives reduce concern about future reputation, but because responders only accept positive-surplus trades anyway, acceptable final deals still need to be individually beneficial.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear latent comparative-advantage structure even though the players do not observe one another's exact values. Buffalo values RED most highly ($0.90) and BLUE least ($0.10). Duck has the mirror pattern, valuing BLUE most highly ($0.90) and RED only moderately ($0.40). Cat sits in the middle, valuing GREEN at the common $0.50, RED at $0.40, and BLUE at $0.20, so cat is the least natural specialist but can still profit by turning low-value BLUE into either RED or GREEN if the price is favorable. Since GREEN is worth the same to everyone, it works as a relatively safe trading currency, but because nobody has a private edge on GREEN, proposals involving GREEN must still respect recipients' opportunity costs. The strongest mutually beneficial trades are therefore buffalo giving BLUE for RED, duck giving RED for BLUE, and cat offering GREEN or RED to get BLUE. The main uncertainty is informational and strategic: each proposer sees only their own calculator, so they can identify what helps themselves but must guess what another player might accept. Because recipients also see exact private gains or losses for their side, unprofitable offers should usually be declined. Given the personas, buffalo is likely to make structured, moderate offers rather than extreme gambits; cat is somewhat inequality-tolerant and not strongly punitive, so she may test opportunistic but still plausible proposals; duck is cognitively strong, not highly exploitation-cautious, and likely to recognize favorable exchange rates quickly. Overall, the most likely pattern is early probing, then convergence toward buffalo-duck RED/BLUE exchange and cat attempting to convert BLUE into more valuable chips whenever possible.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are still learning from sparse public behavior, so proposals are likely to be exploratory rather than perfectly efficient. Buffalo should already know that giving up BLUE is cheap personally and may attract acceptance if priced generously enough. Cat is likely to search for a deal that upgrades BLUE into RED or GREEN. Duck, who values BLUE very highly, has a strong incentive to offer RED for BLUE once he gets a turn, because that trade is privately attractive to him and plausibly attractive to buffalo.

After round 0, the public history reveals that buffalo accepted giving BLUE for RED, which signals that BLUE may be relatively expendable for buffalo and that duck can successfully transact on that margin. Holdings now matter: buffalo has fewer BLUE and more RED, while duck has more BLUE and fewer RED. That makes another buffalo-duck deal possible but slightly tighter. Cat has seen at least one RED-BLUE exchange succeed and may now target buffalo's remaining BLUE, since BLUE is still cat's least valuable chip after GREEN is fixed and cat can profitably exchange GREEN or RED for enough BLUE.

By the final round, players have a clearer sense of what trades can clear. Buffalo now has even fewer BLUE after trading some to cat, so buffalo's best remaining opportunities likely involve trying to convert a little BLUE into RED if possible, but duck may no longer find the same terms attractive given his reduced RED stock. Cat now holds extra BLUE obtained cheaply and should be motivated to turn some of it into RED, which is worth twice as much to cat as BLUE. Duck, with accumulated BLUE and reduced RED, may have fewer profitable offers left and could either make a conservative final attempt or see his proposal declined if it asks too much.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should revolve around private gains from exchanging low-value colors for high-value colors, with green acting as a neutral benchmark because it is worth $0.50 to everyone. The strongest objective complementarities are: butterfly values BLUE very little ($0.10) and RED below green ($0.30), so butterfly most wants to acquire GREEN and especially avoid giving it away cheaply; kangaroo highly values RED ($0.70) and values BLUE only slightly ($0.20), so kangaroo most wants RED and is comparatively willing to part with BLUE; squid highly values BLUE ($0.80) and values RED somewhat below green ($0.40), so squid most wants BLUE and is comparatively willing to part with RED. From an observer perspective, the surplus-improving cycle is therefore RED moving toward kangaroo, BLUE moving toward squid, and GREEN often serving as the easiest commonly acceptable payment medium. However, players only know their own values, so they must infer others' willingness from observed acceptances and holdings. Because responders have exact accept/decline calculators for their own payoffs, trades with negative value for responders should almost never be accepted. That makes accepted trades likely to be individually rational for both sides and usually involve green or a color the responder personally values weakly. Persona priors suggest butterfly is relatively patient and structured, likely to make clean, safe offers rather than gamble wildly; kangaroo is cautious and self-protective, likely to avoid generous proposals and ask aggressively for red; squid is adaptive and relatively cooperative, likely to recognize practical exchange opportunities and accept clear gains. Across three rounds, uncertainty mainly concerns whether proposers can discover counterpart-specific acceptable ratios without direct communication, and whether they risk offers that accidentally attract the wrong accepter when multiple players could in principle say yes.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. In the first round, players have no revealed information except common ranges and their own private values, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and conservative. Butterfly should be inclined to turn low-value BLUE into GREEN, because that is a clear own-payoff improvement and green is a safe, universally valuable asset. Kangaroo should test for ways to acquire RED, probably paying with BLUE because BLUE is kangaroo's weakest color. Squid, who values BLUE most, is likely to seek BLUE and can profitably offer RED for it. Early accepts reveal who finds which ratios attractive.

By round 1, some structure is visible from the public history. Butterfly has shown willingness to trade away BLUE for GREEN and then to accept RED in exchange for BLUE, which is consistent with BLUE being relatively unimportant. Kangaroo learned that offering BLUE for RED at too stingy a ratio failed. Squid successfully acquired BLUE by paying RED, suggesting a plausible direction for future trades. Holdings now also matter: butterfly has more RED and less BLUE than initially, kangaroo has more BLUE and less GREEN, and squid has more BLUE and less RED. These inventories make green-for-red trades especially plausible between butterfly and kangaroo, because butterfly values green above red while kangaroo values red above green.

In the final round, players can target deals more precisely from revealed behavior. Butterfly and kangaroo have already completed mutually beneficial red-green exchanges, so both know that channel can work. Squid has succeeded in buying blue with red before, but after inventory changes squid may not find enough counterpart demand at earlier ratios and could experiment with green as payment. Endgame logic also reduces the value of waiting: if a trade is individually profitable now, there is less reason to hold out for future turns. That said, responders still reject any offer that is negative on their private calculator, so final-round efficiency remains limited by private-value mismatch.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has a clear gains-from-trade structure driven by opposing red and blue valuations. Bat values BLUE most ($0.60) and RED least ($0.20), Fox values BLUE most of all ($1.00) but also likes RED ($0.60), and Frog values RED most ($0.70) while valuing BLUE least ($0.30). GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so green mainly serves as a neutral medium of exchange rather than a source of comparative advantage. The biggest bilateral surplus opportunities are therefore Bat giving RED to Frog in exchange for BLUE, and Frog giving BLUE to Fox in exchange for RED. Bat can also profit by giving RED for GREEN, because Bat dislikes red and values green more, while Frog may accept green-for-red if the ratio is favorable enough. Fox is in a somewhat stronger bargaining position because Fox values both BLUE and RED relatively highly, so Fox is choosier about surrendering BLUE and may prefer acquiring RED cheaply from Bat or Frog. Because players only know their own values during play, they must infer others' willingness to trade from responses and the public holdings path. That creates uncertainty about whether a seemingly generous exchange will be accepted and by whom if two players could both gain. The personas suggest relatively cooperative, non-punitive behavior, so proposals are likely to be value-seeking but not extremely aggressive. Early rounds should feature exploratory offers that test whether others will swap high-value chips at favorable rates; once one or two trades reveal likely comparative advantages, later rounds should become more targeted. A likely full-game path is that Bat successfully trades away low-value RED for BLUE from Frog, Fox then also extracts BLUE value by offering RED to Frog, and later Bat converts remaining RED into GREEN from Fox once blue becomes scarcer in Frog's inventory.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, everyone begins symmetrically with 10 of each chip, but incentives are already asymmetric. Bat has the strongest reason to shed RED, ideally for BLUE, and Frog is the natural counterparty because Frog values RED highly and BLUE poorly. Fox also wants BLUE badly, but giving up BLUE is expensive for Fox, so Fox is more likely to offer RED or GREEN rather than BLUE. Because no one yet knows others' exact values, first-round proposals are likely to be moderate tests of the market rather than extreme one-sided demands.

After round 0, Bat has already improved by converting low-value RED into BLUE, while Frog has deepened specialization toward RED and reduced BLUE holdings. Fox has seen that RED-BLUE trades are feasible and now has better evidence that Frog is a likely RED buyer. Because Bat's RED stock is falling and Frog's BLUE stock is shrinking, trades may become smaller and more selective. Green remains a plausible fallback medium if direct red-blue terms no longer clear.

By the final round, holdings are no longer balanced and the easiest high-surplus trades have mostly been exploited. Bat still benefits from unloading RED whenever possible; Fox still wants valuable chips but is careful about giving up BLUE; Frog now holds a large amount of RED, making additional red purchases less urgent. Final-round behavior is therefore likely to shift toward smaller cleanup trades or rejected asks that are too favorable to the proposer. Since there is no future beyond this round, players may also make slightly more ambitious proposals, but acceptance still depends entirely on immediate private surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: The main surplus pattern is straightforward from an analyst view: dog-green-8079 and turtle-red-9966 both value BLUE at $1.00 and RED at $0.50, while parrot-pink-5400 values RED slightly more at $0.60 and BLUE much less at $0.30. GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it is a neutral transfer chip rather than a source of private-value gains. This creates the clearest bilateral gains between parrot and either of the other two through trades where parrot gives RED and receives BLUE: each BLUE moved from parrot to dog or turtle would destroy value, while each RED moved from dog or turtle to parrot creates only a modest gain for parrot, so parrot is naturally a buyer of RED and seller of BLUE only if terms are favorable enough. But from each player's private perspective during play, they do not know others' values, so they infer willingness from acceptances and from what kinds of offers get posted. Because dog and turtle have identical values, they will often react similarly to posted offers, creating competition to accept attractive deals when parrot offers BLUE for RED or RED for BLUE at favorable rates. Dog's persona suggests relatively flexible, inequality-tolerant bargaining and weak resistance to uneven splits, so dog is likely to test ambitious proposals early. Parrot is prosocial and adaptive but also likes predictability, so parrot is likely to make simpler, calculator-positive offers rather than highly speculative ones. Turtle is more guarded against exploitation and likely to accept only clearly positive deals. Overall, the likely game path is: some early probing by dog that fails because the requested chips are too valuable to others relative to the offer; then a successful parrot trade with one of the BLUE-lovers; then later turns adjust to updated holdings, with proposers using simple positive-surplus trades and avoiding offers that would obviously be rejected. Since multiple acceptors are resolved randomly, attractive offers to dog and turtle can generate recipient uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is likely to feature discovery and anchoring. Dog moves first without public history and is likely to test whether someone will part with BLUE or RED for a modest amount of GREEN or RED. Because GREEN has only common value and turtle is cautious, overly aggressive dog offers are likely declined. Parrot then has a good opportunity to propose a simple trade that is definitely profitable for parrot and plausibly attractive to one BLUE-valuing counterpart. Turtle, moving last, can condition on two observed turns and may prefer a conservative, clearly favorable offer if holdings allow.

By Round 1, everyone has seen that parrot was willing to give RED to obtain BLUE, and both dog and turtle were willing to accept that kind of trade. That public signal makes BLUE appear attractive and RED relatively offerable, though players still do not know exact values. Dog now has slightly fewer BLUE and more RED if chosen as recipient in Round 0, which can make dog more interested in rebuilding BLUE. Parrot, having already improved through one trade, is likely to continue using RED-for-BLUE offers but perhaps at a slightly less generous rate. Turtle remains guarded and will probably only accept if the calculator shows a clean gain.

In the final round, there is no continuation value from signaling, so proposals are likely to be more directly surplus-seeking. Dog may make one last attempt to turn lower-valued RED or common-value GREEN into BLUE. Parrot, if still holding enough RED, remains the most natural proposer of mutually acceptable exchanges with the BLUE-valuers, though stock constraints matter after prior trades. Turtle's final move is likely cautious and opportunistic rather than expansive, especially given high exploitation caution and the short horizon.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game has one clear private-value asymmetry: unicorn-blue-7096 values RED at $0.50 while badger-green-4722 and peacock-purple-5231 value RED at only $0.30; all three value BLUE at $0.10 and GREEN at $0.50. That makes RED especially attractive only to unicorn, while GREEN is universally valuable and BLUE is universally weak. Because players know only their own values during play, badger and peacock are likely to view RED as a relatively cheap asset they can give up, and all players are likely to want GREEN. The easiest mutually acceptable trades therefore tend to be trades where someone gives multiple BLUE for some GREEN, or where badger/peacock give RED in exchange for GREEN if the recipient happens not to value RED highly. But unicorn privately values RED as much as GREEN, so unicorn is much more willing than the others to accept GREEN-for-RED trades and much less willing to give away RED cheaply. The bargaining uncertainty comes from partner selection and hidden values: a proposer cannot target a specific responder, and if both responders accept, the recipient is random. That weakens attempts at fine-tuned bilateral bargaining and pushes toward broadly acceptable offers. Persona priors suggest peacock is the most strategically precise and socially adaptive, badger is more structure-seeking and may make simple calculator-driven offers, and unicorn is cooperative but not strongly fairness-enforcing, so unicorn may accept profitable but unequal trades without much resistance. Overall, the likely path is early testing of GREEN-for-RED or GREEN-for-BLUE exchanges, at least one successful trade with unicorn as counterparty, and later proposals adjusting to updated holdings while still centered on acquiring GREEN and shedding low-value BLUE.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. Since GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone and BLUE only $0.10, proposals asking for GREEN must usually compensate with several BLUE or with some RED. Badger and peacock both value RED at $0.30, so they may be willing to spend RED to get GREEN. Unicorn privately values RED at $0.50, making RED unusually attractive from unicorn's perspective. In this first round, players are likely to probe with simple, calculator-favorable offers rather than highly aggressive demands, because no trade history yet reveals who is likely to accept.

After round 0, badger has more BLUE but fewer GREEN, peacock has gained GREEN and shed BLUE, and unicorn has gained RED while giving up some GREEN. The public history shows that both responders were willing to accept a strong BLUE-for-GREEN exchange, and unicorn accepted RED-for-GREEN from badger. That makes BLUE-for-GREEN look like a reliable mechanism when the offer ratio is generous enough. Unicorn also now has even more RED, which matters because unicorn privately values RED highly and may be increasingly reluctant to part with GREEN unless compensated well.

By the final round, the public pattern suggests that GREEN commands a substantial premium over BLUE, while even one-for-one RED-for-GREEN proposals from unicorn have failed because badger and peacock do not value RED enough. Final-round proposals are therefore likely to become more calculator-driven and opportunistic: badger and peacock may continue converting BLUE into GREEN when possible, while unicorn may either seek a modest profitable GREEN purchase with extra RED or make one last attempt at a broad offer hoping someone accepts despite asymmetric values. With only one round left, there is less reason to hold chips for future bargaining, so profitable acceptances remain likely when the offered side is clearly favorable.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This game should center on asymmetric gains from red and blue chips, with green acting mostly as a neutral exchange medium because it is worth $0.50 to everyone. Dog values BLUE most ($1.00) and also likes RED ($0.80), so dog is naturally willing to give up GREEN to acquire either colored chip and is unlikely to part with BLUE cheaply. Mouse values RED very little ($0.20) and BLUE somewhat ($0.60), so mouse has the strongest incentive to sell RED and try to acquire BLUE or GREEN. Owl values BLUE highly ($1.00) but RED only $0.30, so owl, like mouse, is a natural seller of RED and a reluctant seller of BLUE. Because players only know their own values during play, they must infer from behavior which offers others may accept; however, the interface tells each responder exactly whether a posted trade helps them, so individually losing offers should usually be declined. The most likely successful trades therefore involve mouse or owl giving away RED in exchange for GREEN, and possibly dog offering GREEN for RED if dog wants a safe, clearly attractive deal for the other side. Trades asking for BLUE are much harder to complete because both dog and owl value BLUE at the top of the range, while mouse will also not want to sell BLUE for too little. Persona priors suggest all three are fairly cooperative and not highly punitive, so proposals may be moderately fair rather than aggressively extractive, but self-interest still dominates because each player sees exact private surplus. Overall, the likely path is a few accepted RED-for-GREEN deals, mostly benefiting dog if dog chooses to exploit dog's unusually high RED value, with substantial uncertainty about partner choice whenever multiple responders could profit.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start everyone holds 10 of each chip, so no inventory constraints bind. Early in the game, proposers are likely to test simple offers with green as payment because green has common value and is easy to use as a bargaining yardstick. Dog has the biggest private reason to buy red from either mouse or owl, while mouse has the clearest reason to sell red. Since responders accept only when their own calculator shows a gain, conservative first-round offers that visibly help some unknown counterpart are the most likely to succeed.

After round 0, dog has accumulated extra red and given up some green, mouse has shed some low-value red, and owl has converted red into green once. Public history now reveals that red-for-green trades can clear, while blue remains difficult to pry away. Dog is likely to continue using green to buy red when terms are favorable. Mouse may persist in trying to turn low-value red into something better, but should avoid offers that require others to surrender blue cheaply. Owl, seeing dog accept red-for-green before, may again target dog with a similar proposal.

By the final round, the revealed pattern strongly suggests that dog is the main buyer of red, while mouse and owl are willing sellers when green is offered at roughly fair rates. End-game incentives reduce any reason to preserve chips for later, so proposers may become slightly more direct. Still, offers involving blue should remain unlikely to clear because no obvious low-value blue holder exists. The most likely closing sequence is one more successful dog purchase of red, another failed attempt by mouse to leverage red into blue, and a final owl proposal that dog accepts if the green cost is not too high.

parsed_target.game_explanation: The core efficiency pattern in this game is that rabbit-yellow-6681 values RED much more than the others ($1.00 versus $0.60 for alligator-orange-6315 and $0.50 for buffalo-purple-4069), while alligator-orange-6315 values BLUE the most ($0.50 versus $0.30 for rabbit-yellow-6681 and $0.20 for buffalo-purple-4069). GREEN is common value at $0.50 for everyone, so it is a natural medium of exchange but creates no direct comparative advantage by itself. This means the most plausible mutually beneficial trades are ones that move RED toward rabbit-yellow-6681 and BLUE toward alligator-orange-6315, usually with GREEN or lower-valued chips offered in return. The strategic complication is private information: players know only their own values, so they must infer counterpart incentives from acceptance patterns and trade history. Early proposals are therefore likely to probe the market with modest, favorable-for-sender offers. Because buffalo-purple-4069 and rabbit-yellow-6681 have relatively cooperative and less punitive personas, they may accept small positive-surplus deals rather than hold out aggressively, while alligator-orange-6315's strong reasoning and lower norm-enforcement make him likely to search for efficient but self-serving trades. Another source of uncertainty is that if both non-senders would accept a proposal, the recipient is chosen randomly, so proposers have some incentive to target terms that are attractive to only one likely partner. Across the three rounds, the likely path is gradual discovery: initial testing, then more tailored proposals once players observe who accepts what kinds of exchanges.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no revealed information beyond their own values, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat optimistic for the sender. alligator-orange-6315 should be most interested in acquiring BLUE, but cannot know who values BLUE least; buffalo-purple-4069 values BLUE especially little and is therefore a natural eventual source. buffalo-purple-4069, who values RED and GREEN equally but BLUE least, has an incentive to trade away BLUE for either RED or GREEN if the terms are favorable. rabbit-yellow-6681 strongly values RED and may test whether others are willing to part with it cheaply, but because nobody knows that rabbit-yellow-6681 has the highest RED valuation, the first-round market may underprice RED relative to rabbit-yellow-6681's willingness to pay.

By round 1, the trade history gives informative clues. alligator-orange-6315 has seen buffalo-purple-4069 part with BLUE, suggesting buffalo-purple-4069 does not value BLUE highly. buffalo-purple-4069 has seen rabbit-yellow-6681 accept BLUE in exchange for RED and then actively seek more RED, which can signal that rabbit-yellow-6681 values RED relatively strongly or at least is willing to trade substantial value for it. rabbit-yellow-6681 has learned that buffalo-purple-4069 is willing to release RED for GREEN-heavy packages. This should make proposals more targeted: alligator-orange-6315 should continue trying to accumulate BLUE at favorable rates, buffalo-purple-4069 may keep converting BLUE into higher-valued chips, and rabbit-yellow-6681 may push harder to obtain RED, perhaps from alligator-orange-6315 if the terms are sweetened enough.

In the final round, there is no future strategic value to waiting, so proposals should focus on immediate surplus. By now, alligator-orange-6315 has likely learned that trading RED away for enough BLUE can be profitable, and rabbit-yellow-6681 has repeatedly revealed a strong willingness to acquire RED. buffalo-purple-4069 has likely learned that BLUE is hard to exchange one-for-one into more valuable chips unless the terms strongly favor the counterpart. Final-round behavior should therefore feature more direct exploitation of discovered gains from trade, especially between alligator-orange-6315 and rabbit-yellow-6681. At the same time, holdings constraints matter because earlier accepted trades change inventories, so some otherwise attractive deals may no longer be feasible.
